full_context: The altitude correction factor compensates for differences in external insulation strength caused by atmospheric changes at different altitudes. The formula applies to external insulation coordination withstand voltage correction for equipment at 1000 m and below. The value of q depends on the voltage type (take 1.0 for lightning impulse; for switching impulse, refer to Fig. B.1 based on the coordination withstand voltage).

variable_definitions.name: Altitude correction factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Coefficient correcting external insulation strength variation with altitude

variable_definitions.name: Exponent
variable_definitions.meaning: Parameter related to voltage type

variable_definitions.name: Altitude height
variable_definitions.meaning: Altitude of the equipment installation site

potential_questions: Calculate the lightning impulse correction factor for equipment at 2000 m
Find the switching impulse correction factor at 3000 m for 750 kV
task_description: 1. Determine the voltage type and select \(q\) (1.0 for lightning impulse; for switching impulse, check Fig. B.1). 2. Obtain the installation altitude \(H\) (m). 3. Compute the exponent term \(q\times H/8150\). 4. Take the exponential with base \(e\). 5. Output \(K_a\)

formula:
U_{rp} = U_{pl} + 2ST when U_{pl} \geqslant 2ST 
U_{rp} = 2U_{pl} when U_{pl} < 2ST
full_context: This formula estimates the representative lightning overvoltage magnitude on substation equipment under arrester protection, considering arrester protection level, incoming-wave steepness, and connection distance. It is used for insulation coordination design of range I and II equipment.

variable_definitions.name: Representative overvoltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Lightning overvoltage magnitude on equipment

variable_definitions.name: Arrester lightning impulse protection level
variable_definitions.meaning: Residual voltage of the arrester at nominal discharge current

variable_definitions.name: Incoming-wave steepness
variable_definitions.meaning: Rate of change of the lightning-wave front

variable_definitions.name: Lightning-wave propagation time
variable_definitions.meaning: Propagation time between arrester and equipment

variable_definitions.name: Total distance
variable_definitions.meaning: Electrical distance from arrester to equipment

variable_definitions.name: Speed of light
variable_definitions.meaning: Electromagnetic wave propagation speed

potential_questions: Given U_{pl}=480 kV, S=1200 kV/μs, L=30 m, find the equipment overvoltage
Compute the critical distance so the overvoltage does not exceed 650 kV
task_description: 1. Determine the arrester protection level \(U_{pl}\) (kV). 2. Compute total distance \(L=a_1+a_2+a_3+a_4\) (m). 3. Compute \(T=L/300\) (μs). 4. Estimate incoming-wave steepness \(S\) (kV/μs). 5. Check whether \(U_{pl} \ge 2ST\). 6. Select the corresponding formula to calculate \(U_{rp}\). 7. Output the result (kV)

full_context: Simplified statistical method to compute equipment coordination withstand voltage under lightning overvoltage, suitable for substation insulation coordination design. The formula considers line lightning factor (A), number of incoming lines (n), arrester distance (L), and line span (L_{sp}).

variable_definitions.name: Coordination withstand voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Voltage level the equipment should withstand

variable_definitions.name: Line lightning factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Parameter characterizing line lightning performance

variable_definitions.name: Number of incoming lines
variable_definitions.meaning: Number of lines connected to the substation

variable_definitions.name: Arrester distance
variable_definitions.meaning: Distance from arrester to equipment

variable_definitions.name: Span
variable_definitions.meaning: Line span

variable_definitions.name: Equivalent fault length
variable_definitions.meaning: Line length whose trip rate equals the acceptable fault rate

potential_questions: For a double-circuit incoming line (A=7000 kV) with L=30 m, compute the coordination withstand voltage
task_description: 1. Determine the line type and find \(A\) (kV). 2. Count the number of incoming lines \(n\). 3. Measure the arrester distance \(L\) (m). 4. Obtain the span \(L_{sp}\) (m). 5. Compute \(L_a = R_a/R_{km}\) (m). 6. Compute the fraction \(A\cdot L/(n\cdot(L_{sp}+L_a))\). 7. Compute \(U_{cw} = U_{pl} +\) the fraction (kV)

full_context: Representative inter-phase overvoltage calculation formula for insulation coordination of range II equipment (≥252 kV). It accounts for the influence of inter-phase insulation strength characteristic (B value) on overvoltage components by adjusting with F coefficients.
task_description: 1. Determine the system 2% phase-to-phase overvoltage \(U_{p2}\) (kV). 2. Determine the 2% phase-to-ground overvoltage \(U_{e2}\) (kV). 3. Obtain the insulation characteristic \(B\). 4. Compute \(F_1=0.456\) and \(F_2=0.085\) (when \(B=0.6\)). 5. Compute \(U_{p2re} = 2\times(F_1\times U_{p2} + F_2\times U_{e2})\). 6. Output the result (kV)

full_context: Failure risk rate formula under multi-peak overvoltages, used to evaluate the cumulative insulation risk from multiple independent overvoltage peaks in one operation. Applicable to statistical methods for slow-front overvoltage insulation coordination.

variable_definitions.name: Total failure risk rate
variable_definitions.meaning: Probability of insulation failure

variable_definitions.name: Single-peak failure risk rate
variable_definitions.meaning: Failure probability caused by one overvoltage peak

potential_questions: Given peak risks 0.01, 0.008, and 0.015 for one phase, find the total risk
Compute the single-peak risk upper limit to keep total risk < 0.02
task_description: 1. Determine the risk rates \(R_1\), \(R_2\), \(R_3\) for each overvoltage peak. 2. Compute \((1-R_1)\times(1-R_2)\times(1-R_3)\). 3. Compute \(R = 1 -\) that product. 4. Output the total failure risk rate \(R\)

full_context: The rated current through winding line terminals is derived from transformer rated power (S_r) and rated voltage (U_r). For a three-phase transformer winding, I_r = S_r / (√3 × U_r). The formula applies to current calculation at three-phase transformer line terminals.

variable_definitions.name: Rated current
variable_definitions.meaning: Current flowing through the winding line terminals

variable_definitions.name: Rated apparent power
variable_definitions.meaning: Specified apparent power of the winding

variable_definitions.name: Rated voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Voltage between winding line terminals

potential_questions: Compute the rated current of a 100 MVA, 220 kV three-phase transformer
task_description: 1. Obtain rated apparent power \(S_r\) (VA). 2. Obtain rated voltage \(U_r\) (V). 3. Compute \(S_r\) divided by \((\sqrt{3}\times U_r)\). 4. Output the rated current \(I_r\) (A)

full_context: When the current is K times the rated current (0 ≤ K ≤ 1), the over-excitation limit requires the voltage-to-frequency ratio not to exceed 110 − 5K (%). It is used to evaluate safe operation of transformers under non-rated frequency and voltage.

variable_definitions.name: Operating voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Actual applied voltage

variable_definitions.name: Rated voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Rated voltage of the winding (same as in 3.4.7)

variable_definitions.name: Operating frequency
variable_definitions.meaning: Actual supply frequency

variable_definitions.name: Rated frequency
variable_definitions.meaning: Design frequency of the transformer

variable_definitions.name: Load factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of actual current to rated current

potential_questions: When K=0.8, calculate the maximum allowable U/f ratio
task_description: 1. Obtain the operating voltage \(U\). 2. Obtain the rated voltage \(U_r\). 3. Obtain the operating frequency \(f\). 4. Obtain the rated frequency \(f_r\). 5. Determine the load factor \(K\). 6. Compute \((U/U_r)\times(f_r/f)\times 100\). 7. Compute \(110 - 5K\). 8. Verify that the inequality holds

full_context: Correction formula for measured no-load loss P_m: P_0 = P_m(1+d), where d = (U − U')/U'. It is used to correct measurement error caused by supply voltage waveform distortion; |U' − U|/U' must not exceed 3%.

variable_definitions.name: Corrected no-load loss
variable_definitions.meaning: No-load loss after waveform distortion correction

variable_definitions.name: Measured no-load loss
variable_definitions.meaning: Directly measured no-load loss

variable_definitions.name: Correction factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Correction term for waveform distortion

variable_definitions.name: RMS voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Reading of the RMS voltmeter

variable_definitions.name: Average voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Reading of the average voltmeter (scaled to RMS of a sine wave)

potential_questions: Given P_m=50 kW, U=10.2 kV, and U'=10 kV, compute the corrected no-load loss
task_description: 1. Measure the average voltage \(U'\). 2. Measure the RMS voltage \(U\). 3. Compute \(d = (U - U')/U'\). 4. Measure the no-load loss \(P_m\). 5. Compute \(P_0 = P_m\times(1+d)\). 6. Output the corrected no-load loss

full_context: Hot-spot temperature rise calculation for windings: Δθ_h = Δθ_o + H × g. Used to estimate the hottest-spot temperature rise of oil-immersed transformer windings. H is the hot-spot factor (product of Q and s), and g is the average winding temperature gradient.

variable_definitions.name: Hot-spot temperature rise
variable_definitions.meaning: Temperature difference between the winding hot spot and the cooling medium

variable_definitions.name: Top-oil temperature rise
variable_definitions.meaning: Temperature difference between top oil and the cooling medium

variable_definitions.name: Hot-spot factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Local temperature-rise amplification factor (H = Q×s)

variable_definitions.name: Average winding temperature gradient
variable_definitions.meaning: Difference between average winding temperature and average oil temperature

potential_questions: Given Δθ_o = 45 K, g = 25 K, and H = 1.8, compute the hot-spot temperature rise
task_description: 1. Measure the top-oil temperature rise \(\Delta\theta_o\). 2. Measure the average winding temperature gradient \(g\). 3. Determine the hot-spot factor \(H\). 4. Compute \(\Delta\theta_o + H\times g\). 5. Output the hot-spot temperature rise \(\Delta\theta_h\)

full_context: Winding average temperature cooling curve: θ_w(t) = A_0 − k t + g × e^{−t/T_w}. It is used to extrapolate the winding average temperature at the moment of power-off. A_0 is the initial estimate of the average liquid temperature, k is the linear decay slope, and T_w is the winding time constant.

variable_definitions.name: Winding average temperature
variable_definitions.meaning: Winding average temperature at time t

variable_definitions.name: Constant term
variable_definitions.meaning: Initial estimate of average liquid temperature

variable_definitions.name: Decay slope
variable_definitions.meaning: Linear decay rate of liquid temperature

variable_definitions.name: Temperature gradient
variable_definitions.meaning: Winding-to-oil temperature difference at power-off (same as Appendix D)

variable_definitions.name: Winding time constant
variable_definitions.meaning: Exponential decay time constant of winding temperature

variable_definitions.name: Time
variable_definitions.meaning: Time after power-off

potential_questions: Given A_0 = 80℃, k = 0.5℃/min, g = 10 K, and T_w = 5 min, find the winding temperature at t = 2 min
task_description: 1. Determine parameters \(A_0\), \(k\), \(g\), and \(T_w\). 2. Specify time \(t\). 3. Compute the linear term \(A_0 - k\times t\). 4. Compute the exponential term \(g\times e^{-t/T_w}\). 5. Sum to obtain \(\theta_w(t)\)

full_context: Transformers shall provide measured insulation resistance; tests are usually performed at 5℃–40℃ with relative humidity below 85%. This is a routine test. When test temperatures differ, insulation resistance can be converted by R₂ = R₁ × 1.5^{(t₁ − t₂)/10}. The formula is used for temperature correction of insulation resistance for oil-immersed power transformers.

variable_definitions.name: Insulation resistance at t₁
variable_definitions.meaning: Measured insulation resistance at the initial temperature

variable_definitions.name: Insulation resistance at t₂
variable_definitions.meaning: Converted insulation resistance at the target temperature

variable_definitions.name: Initial measurement temperature
variable_definitions.meaning: Ambient temperature during the first insulation resistance test

variable_definitions.name: Target conversion temperature
variable_definitions.meaning: Ambient temperature to which the insulation resistance is converted

potential_questions: Given t₁ = 30℃ and R₁ = 500 MΩ, find R₂ at t₂ = 20℃
task_description: 1. Measure insulation resistance \(R_1\) at temperature \(t_1\). 2. Determine the target temperature \(t_2\). 3. Compute the temperature difference \((t_1-t_2)\). 4. Compute the exponent \(\frac{t_1-t_2}{10}\). 5. Compute \(1.5\) raised to that exponent. 6. Compute \(R_2 = R_1\times 1.5^{\frac{t_1-t_2}{10}}\). 7. Output \(R_2\)

full_context: Transformers shall provide dielectric loss factor (tanδ); tests are usually performed at 5℃–40℃. This is a routine test. Different temperatures can be converted using tanδ₂ = tanδ₁ × 1.3^{(t₂ − t₁)/10}. The formula is used for temperature correction of dielectric loss for oil-immersed power transformers.

variable_definitions.name: Dielectric loss factor at t₁
variable_definitions.meaning: Measured dielectric loss at the initial temperature

variable_definitions.name: Dielectric loss factor at t₂
variable_definitions.meaning: Converted dielectric loss at the target temperature

variable_definitions.name: Initial measurement temperature
variable_definitions.meaning: Ambient temperature during the first dielectric loss test

variable_definitions.name: Target conversion temperature
variable_definitions.meaning: Ambient temperature to which dielectric loss is converted

potential_questions: Given t₁ = 25℃ and tanδ₁ = 0.02, find tanδ₂ at t₂ = 40℃
task_description: 1. Measure \(\tan\delta_1\) at temperature \(t_1\). 2. Determine the target temperature \(t_2\). 3. Compute the temperature difference \((t_2-t_1)\). 4. Compute the exponent \(\frac{t_2-t_1}{10}\). 5. Compute \(1.3\) raised to that exponent. 6. Compute \(\tan\delta_2 = \tan\delta_1\times 1.3^{\frac{t_2-t_1}{10}}\). 7. Output \(\tan\delta_2\)

full_context: For machines with rated capacity up to and including 1200 MVA, the stator must withstand 1.5 times rated stator current for 30 s without damage. For ≤1200 MVA machines, the allowable overcurrent time versus multiple satisfies (I² − 1)t = 37.5 s, where I is the stator overcurrent per-unit value and t is the duration (applicable range 10–60 s).

variable_definitions.name: Stator overcurrent multiple
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of actual stator current to rated stator current

variable_definitions.name: Allowable duration
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum time the machine can withstand overcurrent

potential_questions: Given overcurrent multiple I = 1.3, find the allowable duration t
task_description: 1. Determine the overcurrent multiple \(I\). 2. Compute \(I^2\). 3. Compute \(I^2-1\). 4. Compute \(t = 37.5/(I^2-1)\). 5. Verify that \(t\) is within 10–60 s. 6. Output \(t\)

full_context: Overcurrent capability of generator field windings: (I² − 1)t = 33.75, where I is the field current per-unit value (actual/rated) and t is the allowable duration (range 10–120 s). The formula describes rotor winding thermal withstand under overcurrent, applicable to salient-pole synchronous generator rotor overcurrent protection design.

variable_definitions.name: Field current multiple
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of actual field current to rated field current

variable_definitions.name: Allowable duration
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum time the rotor can withstand overcurrent

potential_questions: Given field overcurrent I = 1.2, find the allowable duration t
task_description: 1. Determine the field overcurrent multiple \(I\). 2. Compute \(I^2\). 3. Compute \(I^2-1\). 4. Compute \(t = 33.75/(I^2-1)\). 5. Verify that \(t\) is within 10–120 s. 6. Output \(t\)

formula: \Delta T = \begin{cases} 40 - t & \text{for } 10^\circ C \leq t \leq 60^\circ C \\ 30 + 0.5(10 - t) & \text{for } -20^\circ C \leq t < 10^\circ C \end{cases}
full_context: Temperature-rise adjustment formula for indirectly cooled gas-turbine generator windings: when the primary coolant temperature is 10℃–60℃, ΔT = (40 − t) K; when the temperature is −20℃–10℃, ΔT = 30 + 0.5(10 − t) K. This adjustment is added to the temperature-rise limits of Tables E.3/E.4 to correct allowable temperature rise under non-standard coolant temperatures.

variable_definitions.name: Temperature-rise adjustment
variable_definitions.meaning: Correction to be added to the standard temperature-rise limit

variable_definitions.name: Primary coolant temperature
variable_definitions.meaning: Coolant inlet temperature

potential_questions: Given coolant temperature t = 45℃, find ΔT
task_description: 1. Measure the coolant inlet temperature \(t\). 2. Determine which temperature range applies. 3. If \(10^{\circ}C \le t \le 60^{\circ}C\), compute \(\Delta T = 40 - t\). 4. If \(-20^{\circ}C \le t < 10^{\circ}C\), compute \(\Delta T = 30 + 0.5(10 - t)\). 5. Output \(\Delta T\)

formula: \Delta T = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{for } 10^\circ C \leq t \leq 60^\circ C \\ -0.3(10 - t) & \text{for } -20^\circ C < t < 10^\circ C \end{cases}
full_context: Temperature adjustment formula for directly cooled gas-turbine generator windings: no adjustment when the primary coolant temperature is 10℃–60℃; when the temperature is −20℃–10℃, ΔT = −0.3(10 − t) K. This adjustment is subtracted from the temperature limits in Table E.5 to correct allowable temperatures under non-standard coolant temperatures.

variable_definitions.name: Temperature adjustment
variable_definitions.meaning: Correction to be subtracted from the standard temperature limit

variable_definitions.name: Primary coolant temperature
variable_definitions.meaning: Coolant inlet temperature

potential_questions: Given coolant temperature t = 5℃, find ΔT
task_description: 1. Measure the coolant inlet temperature \(t\). 2. Determine which temperature range applies. 3. If \(10^{\circ}C \le t \le 60^{\circ}C\), \(\Delta T = 0\). 4. If \(-20^{\circ}C < t < 10^{\circ}C\), compute \(\Delta T = -0.3(10 - t)\). 5. Output \(\Delta T\)

full_context: Insulation resistance temperature conversion formula for generators: R₂ = R₁ × 1.5^{(t₁ − t₂)/10}. This is a routine test, used to convert insulation resistance values at different temperatures for salient-pole synchronous generators.

variable_definitions.name: Insulation resistance at t₁
variable_definitions.meaning: Measured insulation resistance at the initial temperature

variable_definitions.name: Insulation resistance at t₂
variable_definitions.meaning: Converted insulation resistance at the target temperature

variable_definitions.name: Initial measurement temperature
variable_definitions.meaning: Ambient temperature during the first insulation resistance measurement

variable_definitions.name: Target conversion temperature
variable_definitions.meaning: Ambient temperature for the converted insulation resistance

potential_questions: Given t₁ = 35℃ and R₁ = 1000 MΩ, find R₂ at t₂ = 25℃
task_description: 1. Measure insulation resistance \(R_1\) at temperature \(t_1\). 2. Determine the target temperature \(t_2\). 3. Compute the temperature difference \((t_1-t_2)\). 4. Compute the exponent \(\frac{t_1-t_2}{10}\). 5. Compute \(1.5\) raised to that exponent. 6. Compute \(R_2 = R_1\times 1.5^{\frac{t_1-t_2}{10}}\). 7. Output \(R_2\)

full_context: Dielectric loss factor temperature conversion formula for generators: tanδ₂ = tanδ₁ × 1.3^{(t₂ − t₁)/10}. This is a routine test used to convert dielectric loss factors at different temperatures for salient-pole synchronous generators.

variable_definitions.name: Dielectric loss factor at t₁
variable_definitions.meaning: Measured dielectric loss factor at the initial temperature

variable_definitions.name: Converted dielectric loss factor at t₂
variable_definitions.meaning: Converted dielectric loss factor at the target temperature

variable_definitions.name: Initial measurement temperature
variable_definitions.meaning: Ambient temperature during the first dielectric loss measurement

variable_definitions.name: Target conversion temperature
variable_definitions.meaning: Ambient temperature for the converted dielectric loss factor

potential_questions: Given t₁ = 30℃ and tanδ₁ = 0.015, find tanδ₂ at t₂ = 50℃
task_description: 1. Measure \(\tan\delta_1\) at temperature \(t_1\). 2. Determine the target temperature \(t_2\). 3. Compute the temperature difference \((t_2-t_1)\). 4. Compute the exponent \(\frac{t_2-t_1}{10}\). 5. Compute \(1.3\) raised to that exponent. 6. Compute \(\tan\delta_2 = \tan\delta_1\times 1.3^{\frac{t_2-t_1}{10}}\). 7. Output \(\tan\delta_2\)

full_context: Stator overcurrent capability formula for generators: (I² − 1)t = 37.5, where I is the stator overcurrent multiple (actual/rated) and t is the allowable duration (range 10–60 s). It describes the thermal withstand capability of stator windings under overcurrent, suitable for salient-pole synchronous generator overcurrent protection design.

variable_definitions.name: Stator overcurrent multiple
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of actual stator current to rated stator current

variable_definitions.name: Allowable duration
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum time the stator can withstand overcurrent

potential_questions: Given overcurrent multiple I = 1.4, find the allowable duration t
task_description: 1. Determine the stator overcurrent multiple \(I\). 2. Compute \(I^2\). 3. Compute \(I^2-1\). 4. Compute \(t = 37.5/(I^2-1)\). 5. Verify that \(t\) is within 10–60 s. 6. Output \(t\)

full_context: Rotor overcurrent capability formula for generators: (I² − 1)t = 33.75, where I is the field current multiple (actual/rated) and t is the allowable duration (range 10–120 s). It describes the thermal withstand capability of rotor field windings under overcurrent, suitable for salient-pole synchronous generator rotor overcurrent protection design.

variable_definitions.name: Field current multiple
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of actual field current to rated field current

variable_definitions.name: Allowable duration
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum time the rotor can withstand overcurrent

potential_questions: Given field overcurrent I = 1.25, find the allowable duration t
task_description: 1. Determine the field overcurrent multiple \(I\). 2. Compute \(I^2\). 3. Compute \(I^2-1\). 4. Compute \(t = 33.75/(I^2-1)\). 5. Verify that \(t\) is within 10–120 s. 6. Output \(t\)
